§6Corium§f

§aThe deadly aftermath of a meltdown — molten fuel that burns through everything!§f

§bCorium§f is a highly hazardous material formed during an §bRBMK§f or §bPWR§f meltdown. It is made from molten fuel, cladding, and reactor structures. It does not occur naturally.

§6Properties§f:
§b• Radioactivity§f: §e150 RAD/s§f
§b• NFPA 704§f: Health §e4§f, Fire §e0§f, Reactivity §e3§f

§6Creation§f: Forms when an RBMK or PWR melts down. The amount of corium created depends on reactor size — smaller reactors produce less.

§6Behavior§f: Corium melts through the ground and penetrates any barrier in its path. It is highly resistant to mining and explosions, making cleanup extremely difficult.

§6Uses§f: Currently none. Corium has no known refining or recycling method. It simply radiates the environment.

§c⚠ Safety§f: The cleanup of even a small RBMK meltdown requires heavy radiation protection gear. Corium is the most persistent contaminant — plan your reactor designs carefully to avoid ever needing to deal with it!
